chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: The overall toxicity is considered mild1. The toxin is primarily concentrated within the plant's milky sap
chemicals.description: in cats: Ficin is a proteolytic (protein-breaking) enzyme that acts as a physical irritant. Upon contact or ingestion, it causes irritation to the mucous membranes of the mouth and the gastrointestinal tract, leading to drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea1. It is also implicated in causing contact dermatitis (redness and irritation) if the sap gets on the cat's skin
chemicals.name: Ficin

chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: These compounds are localized in the plant's sap23. The overall potency is mild, though the sap can cause notable discomfort to the skin and digestive system
chemicals.description: in cats: Similar to ficin, these compounds are irritants. If ingested or touched, they contribute to the oral irritation, gastrointestinal distress, and contact dermatitis experienced by the cat
chemicals.name: Psoralen (also referred to as ficusin), along with other furocoumarins, sesquiterpenoid glycosides, and triterpenes

description:
Rubber Plant (Ficus elastica) is a large-leafed houseplant with thick, glossy leaves. It is not safe for cats to chew, because the leaves and stems release an irritating milky sap when damaged.

The main concern is latex sap that contains ficin and other irritating compounds. Ficin is an enzyme that can bother the mouth and digestive tract. The sap can also irritate the skin or eyes if it touches them, especially when a leaf or stem breaks.

A cat that chews rubber plant may drool, vomit, have diarrhea, lose interest in food, or show mouth discomfort. Sap contact can cause redness, itching, or squinting if it reaches the eye area. Keep rubber plants away from cats, clean up broken leaves, and contact a veterinarian if symptoms are more than mild or do not improve.
name: Rubber Plant
safetyNotes: Main hazard: Milky latex sap in broken leaves and stems can irritate the mouth, stomach, skin, and eyes.
Symptoms: Drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, reduced appetite, mouth discomfort, or skin and eye redness after sap contact.
Exposure risk: The large leaves may invite chewing, and damaged leaves release more sap.
Action: Move the plant out of reach, wipe sap away with water, and call a vet if symptoms are strong or persistent.

symptoms.name: Irritation of the mouth and drooling (hypersalivation)
symptoms.notes: This is caused by the physical and chemical irritation from the proteolytic enzymes (ficin) and psoralen (ficusin) within the plant's sap12. 2. Gastrointestinal Upset (Vomiting and Diarrhea)

symptoms.name: Vomiting and diarrhea
symptoms.notes: If the cat swallows the irritating sap, it leads to mild stomach and intestinal upset1. 3. Contact Dermatitis (Skin Irritation)

symptoms.name: Skin redness and contact dermatitis
symptoms.notes: Contact dermatitis occurs if the plant's milky sap gets on the cat's skin or fur1. The sap contains multiple irritants, including furocoumarins, psoralens, ficin, sesquiterpenoid glycosides, and triterpenes, which are directly implicated in causing this skin reaction

treatments.description: Rinse out the cat's mouth with water and thoroughly wipe off any milky sap from the cat's fur and skin
treatments.name: Rinsing and Sap Removal
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent first step. The plant's sap contains irritating proteolytic enzymes (ficin) and psoralens. Removing the sap immediately stops the localized burning in the mouth, prevents the cat from ingesting more sap while grooming, and helps prevent contact dermatitis on the skin12. 2. Hy

treatments.description: Offer the cat water to drink and monitor them for any ongoing clinical signs, such as continued vomiting, drooling, or skin redness
treatments.name: Hydration and At-Home Monitoring
treatments.notes: Because Rubber Plant toxicity is considered mild, many cases resolve on their own once the irritant is removed. Drinking water helps soothe the irritated mucous membranes in the mouth and throat1. 3. Veterinary Intervention

treatments.description: If the cat experiences persistent vomiting, diarrhea, or severe skin irritation that does not resolve, they should be taken to a veterinarian for professional symptomatic care
treatments.name: Symptomatic and Supportive Care
treatments.notes: While not explicitly detailed for the Rubber Plant in the sources, standard veterinary symptomatic care for persistent GI upset or contact dermatitis typically involves administering anti-nausea medications, fluid therapy for dehydration, or skin-soothing topical treatments
